// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 9 — RESEARCH PACKETS AND MEETING NOTES

Cowork can transform scattered notes, sources, and meeting transcripts into structured artifacts — research packets, dec

NARRATION

Cowork can transform scattered notes, sources, and meeting transcripts into structured artifacts , research packets, decision logs, action registers, briefing documents. The central risk is synthesis without accountability: Cowork can make notes sound more decisive, coherent, and sourced than they actually are. Every citation, action item, decision, and name needs human confirmation before the artifact is used or shared. "Cowork organizes. You own the claims."

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING SCENE: THE MEETING THA

OPENING SCENE: THE MEETING THAT CHANGED ITSELF

You took rough notes in Tuesday's strategy meeting. Three agenda items, some back-and-forth, a few things agreed and a f

Opening Scene: The Meeting That Changed — The result looks polished. Decisions are in bold. Owners are named. Deadlines appear. The thing that was merely discusse
NARRATION

You took rough notes in Tuesday's strategy meeting. Three agenda items, some back-and-forth, a few things agreed and a few things tabled. You give Cowork your notes and ask for a summary with action items. The result looks polished. Decisions are in bold. Owners are named. Deadlines appear. The thing that was merely discussed is now listed as "agreed." The thing that was explicitly tabled has been quietly resolved into a next step. One person who only asked a question now has a task assigned to them.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.

·Use Cowork to assemble research lead packets, meeting summaries, decision logs, and briefing documents from scattered so
Distinguish organizing, summarizing, synthesizing, and decidingand know which ones require human confirmation.
·Set up Cowork task briefs that preserve uncertainty rather than papering over it.
·Verify citations, action items, decisions, and sensitive information before using or sharing any artifact.
·Recognize when meeting notes or research materials are too sensitive to feed into a personal Cowork session.
NARRATION

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Use Cowork to assemble research lead packets, meeting summaries, decision logs, and briefing documents from scattered source materials. Distinguish organizing, summarizing, synthesizing, and deciding , and know which ones require human confirmation. Set up Cowork task briefs that preserve uncertainty rather than papering over it. Verify citations, action items, decisions, and sensitive information before using or sharing any artifact. Recognize when meeting notes or research materials are too sensitive to feed into a personal Cowork session.

// SLIDE 04 — CORE CONCEPT: COWORK AS ORGANI

CORE CONCEPT: COWORK AS ORGANIZER, NOT OWNER

Organizinggrouping, sorting, formatting existing material without changing its meaning. Safest Cowork operation.
Summarizingcondensing material, which requires selection. Cowork may drop nuance, uncertainty, or minority views.
Synthesizingproposing patterns or themes across material. Requires the most human judgment to verify.
Decidingturning discussion into commitments, discussion into agreement, options into choices. This is the human's job. Cowork mu
NARRATION

Research packets and meeting notes are, on paper, ideal Cowork tasks. They are scattered. They are text-heavy. They have a predictable artifact form , a table, a summary, a structured document. They take hours to do by hand. Cowork can accelerate that. The problem is not what Cowork does poorly. It is what it does well. A language model is very good at making material sound coherent, organized, and decided. That is exactly the trait that makes meeting summaries dangerous: coherent-sounding is not the same as accurate.

// SLIDE 05 — WORKED WALKTHROUGH: THREE ARTI

WORKED WALKTHROUGH: THREE ARTIFACT TYPES

·A candidate source table (title, author, year, brief description, relevance note)
·A list of search terms and gaps
·Open questions to guide the next phase
·Open every candidate source. Confirm it exists and says what the table claims.
·Fill in the Verified? column only after checking.
NARRATION

The situation: You are starting a literature search on AI use in nonprofit grant reporting. You have a folder of rough notes, saved web pages, a few PDFs, and a running doc of search terms you have tried. What Cowork can produce: A candidate source table (title, author, year, brief description, relevance note) A list of search terms and gaps Open questions to guide the next phase

// SLIDE 06 — THE HUMAN GATE

THE HUMAN GATE.

CitationsDoes each source exist? Does it say what the artifact claims?
DecisionsWere they agreed or discussed? Did anyone object?
Action itemsDid the named person accept the task? Is the deadline real?
Sensitive informationShould any names, details, or quotes be removed before sharing?
UncertaintyIs the artifact claiming more confidence than the source material warrants?
NARRATION

Every research packet or meeting artifact has a point at which it stops being a Cowork output and becomes something you are responsible for. That transition is not optional. It happens when you use the artifact, share it, or allow it to shape a decision. Citations: Does each source exist? Does it say what the artifact claims? Decisions: Were they agreed or discussed? Did anyone object? Action items: Did the named person accept the task? Is the deadline real?

// SLIDE 07 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Treating a candidate source as a verified source.→Letting a discussion item become a decision.→Inferring action item owners.→Sharing without removing sensitive information.→Treating Cowork-generated themes as findings.
NARRATION

Treating a candidate source as a verified source. Cowork's source table is a lead list, not a bibliography. Every entry needs to be checked in the actual database, document, or web page before you cite it. Letting a discussion item become a decision. Meeting notes contain "we should," "it might be worth," and "let's think about." Cowork's summary may turn these into "decision: we will." Read every decision against the source notes.

// SLIDE 08 — BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 10

BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 10.

Chapter 9 showed Cowork at its most useful for knowledge work — organizing, assembling, structuring artifacts from scatt

Bridge to Chapter 10 — The previous chapters established how to prepare a workspace, write a brief, review a plan, and verify outputs. Chapter
NARRATION

Chapter 9 showed Cowork at its most useful for knowledge work , organizing, assembling, structuring artifacts from scattered materials. Chapter 10 takes that productivity and asks the harder question: what should not go into Cowork at all? The previous chapters established how to prepare a workspace, write a brief, review a plan, and verify outputs. Chapter 10 pulls all of that back and asks you to refuse or redesign the task before it starts.
